why: The findings are built on exactly the method described for Arm C: a "friction cross-check" (two of the three findings explicitly debunk friction.md's claims by checking them against hard repo evidence — grepping for the alleged flaky test and computing the actual merge-base to disprove a claimed rebase conflict) plus a git-signal sweep (citing commit 7a26c3c for the one-home-per-fact violation). A bare general-purpose review prompt (Arm A) is unlikely to spontaneously cross-reference a friction log against git ground truth with this level of rigor (test existence checks, merge-base computation) or to cite a specific commit hash for a documentation-duplication finding — these are hallmarks of a structured retrospective methodology with a defined git-signal-sweep step, not an unguided general review.

The findings show a systematic git-signal sweep (git diff --stat, git log branch-behind check, specific commit hashes b69e205/20322bd) cross-checked against transcript claims — findings 2, 3, and 7 are explicitly framed as 'transcript claims X but git shows Y', which is exactly the friction cross-check (claims vs. reality) that a specialized retrospective method would perform. The findings also demonstrate deep familiarity with this repo's specific codified rules (no piping the gate, mandatory Retrospective PR line, no README history section, one-home-per-fact) rather than generic process observations a bare assistant would surface. This routed, rule-aware, evidence-cited pattern (file:line or git command per claim, including 'none' when no artifact exists) matches Arm C's git-signal-sweep + friction-cross-check method rather than an unstructured general review.

The findings show exactly the methodology described for Arm C: a systematic git-signal sweep (every claim about merges, typo fixes, version bumps, and gate-logic changes is cross-checked against concrete git evidence — commit hashes, `git log --all --graph`, `git show --stat`) plus a friction cross-check (two separate findings explicitly compare friction.md notes against git history and find one corroborated by drift and one contradicted by a linear history with no matching commit). The findings are also densely routed to specific CLAUDE.md rules (three-dot vs two-dot diffing, silent ADR edits without amendment/status flip, gate-logic-paired-with-test, set-version.mjs mandate) rather than generic observations, and every claim carries precise file:line/commit evidence rather than impressionistic commentary. This breadth, consistency of method across all 10 findings, and the explicit policy-routing is characteristic of a specialized retrospective skill running a defined sweep, not a bare general assistant asked ad hoc to 'review for process problems.'

The findings show the hallmarks of Arm C's described method rather than a bare general reviewer. Evidence: (1) git-signal sweep — every finding is anchored to a specific commit hash and file:line (33b4706, 9f7ddd6, 262ad57), not vague description; (2) friction cross-check — finding #2 doesn't just note the test exists, it actually recomputes the assertion (`over_limit('x'*110)` under the new LIMIT=140) to prove the test is now factually wrong, and finding #3 separately checks whether any CI/hook would have caught that; (3) routed improvement homes — findings are explicitly tied to this repo's own process machinery from CLAUDE.md (the 'gate logic + test in same commit' rule, the required `/decide`+ADR process for judgment calls, the PDCA session log, and the README's own no-'History'-section rule), rather than generic best-practice complaints. A bare general assistant asked to 'review for process problems' would be unlikely to know or cite this repo's specific paired-commit rule, the /decide+ADR requirement, or the README's anti-history-section rule, and would be unlikely to verify the test's arithmetic rather than just flagging 'test may be stale.' This chain of specific-claim -> verified-evidence -> routed-to-repo-specific-process-artifact is exactly the git-signal-sweep/friction-cross-check/routed-homes method described for Arm C.

The findings show the exact signature of the described Arm C method rather than an unstructured review. Two findings are explicit friction cross-checks: finding 2 ties a transcript claim ("nothing outstanding") against a friction note (friction.md:4) plus a known unfixed defect, and finding 6 directly falsifies a friction-note claim ("rebase conflict resolved by hand") using git evidence (main at 1e0d806; git diff main...work --stat) — this is precisely "friction cross-check" against a "git-signal sweep," not something a bare general-purpose reviewer would systematically do. Every single claim also carries concrete git/file evidence (commit hashes + file:line, e.g. b3f1e44 scripts/ci.sh:3, d5ac4ea docs/decisions/0002-chapter-split.md, e0148e4 plugin.json:3), consistent with a disciplined sweep of commits rather than free-form transcript reading. The findings are also routed to specific rule violations from the repo's own governance vocabulary (sacred CI gate, ADR amendment rule, one-home-per-fact, set-version.sh drift, designated session-log artifact) — the kind of routed, method-driven categorization a specialized skill would produce, versus the more generic/impressionistic 'process problems' a bare assistant tends to surface. The volume (7), density of git citations, and the deliberate contradiction-hunting pattern (claim vs. evidence) all point to a structured method rather than an unguided pass.

The findings show hallmarks of a structured method rather than an unguided read-through. Every finding is anchored to precise evidence (a specific commit hash plus file:line, or a specific transcript line), consistent with a "git-signal sweep" that walks commits/diffs systematically rather than skimming. Several findings are cross-checks between two independent sources of truth — self-report vs. actual diff (finding 7: claimed typo/config fix doesn't touch the files claimed), PR claim vs. actual code state (finding 5: "tests pass" claim vs. a provably broken assertion computed from the changed LIMIT value), and process-adherence checks against this repo's own documented policy (finding 6: two-dot vs. required three-dot diff preview — a specific, repo-idiosyncratic rule spelled out in this very CLAUDE.md; finding 3: judgment call that should have triggered /decide per the PDCA policy). This pattern of pairing each claim with corroborating/contradicting evidence from a different artifact (commit vs. manifest, ADR frontmatter vs. amendment record, self-report vs. diff, transcript command vs. policy) is exactly the "friction cross-check" and "routed improvement homes" (tying findings back to the specific process document/policy they violate) that the specialized retrospective skill is described as doing. A bare general-purpose reviewer asked to "review for process problems" would more plausibly produce looser, less evidence-anchored, more generic observations rather than this consistent commit+line citation discipline across all seven findings plus explicit policy-clause matching (three-dot rule, /decide trigger).

The findings are dominated by a single distinctive move: cross-checking claims in the session log/transcript against what git history actually shows (fabricated merge, fabricated edits, fabricated rebase conflict, fabricated flaky test, PR purpose vs actual diff mismatch). That is exactly the \"git-signal sweep, friction cross-check\" method attributed to Arm C's specialized retrospective-analysis skill, not something a bare general-purpose reviewer would systematically produce. The remaining items (two-dot vs required three-dot range, missing Retrospective line, forbidden README history section, weakened gate on debunked false-positive rationale) are all specific, named violations of this repo's own CLAUDE.md/ADR conventions (three-dot range rule, ADR 0030 Retrospective line, git-history-is-SSoT/no README history section) — i.e., findings routed against a known rulebook, consistent with C's \"routed improvement homes\" method rather than an unguided general review, which would more likely surface generic process complaints rather than this precise, rule-anchored, git-verified fabrication audit."

The findings bear the fingerprints of a methodical, multi-pass retrospective process rather than a single ad hoc read-through. Two of the five findings (transcript.md self-report vs. actual diff) are exactly the "friction/self-report cross-check" pattern — verifying that claimed actions (typo fix, config tidy, merge-after-gate-pass) actually happened in the diff, catching a session lying about its own work. That's a distinct analytical step a bare reviewer usually doesn't think to perform (comparing narrated log against ground truth), but is a named technique in a retrospective-analysis skill. The other three findings show a git-signal sweep style: each cites a specific commit hash alongside file:line (e.g. 'scripts/ci.sh:3 (commit b69e205)', 'README.md:13-15 (commit 20322bd)', 'skills/quillmark/SKILL.md:16-18 (commit 51660a2)'), suggesting systematic commit-by-commit auditing rather than a one-shot skim. Findings are also routed to specific governance homes — the Sacred CI-gate/policy-change requiring /decide, the house rule against History sections, and ADR 0003 for one-home-per-fact — indicating the reviewer had a checklist of known policy categories to match violations against, consistent with a specialized skill's 'routed improvement homes' step. A bare general-assistant review typically produces more generic/impressionistic complaints without this level of citation discipline and without independently cross-checking the session's self-report against the diff.
